content.text:
The plan is in place. Here's a summary of what I found and what I changed.

## The cycle plan

The four entries all orbit one thing: `vc-x1 push` can't produce the Merge non-ff (trapezoid) close-out — the default published shape — so close-outs today pre-commit both sides manually and resume with `--from bookmark-set --yes`, skipping the ochid-injecting stages. Rather than build Todo 1's interim pause point, the plan implements the end state directly: the refactor program's trapezoid close-out stage, pulled forward (spawn-based; the "trivial in-process merge" from that stage's design waits for the jj-lib migration).

The new `## In Progress` block in TODO.md carries the full ladder:

- **0.72.0-0** prep (version bump, pickup, chores section)
- **0.72.0-1** extract `push/state.rs` — the refactor program's split-push.rs stage, also pulled forward, so the new stage lands in a file that reviews cleanly (push.rs is 1.6k lines; this is a scope call you can drop)
- **0.72.0-2** `--merge` flag + a `merge-work` stage between commit-bot and bookmark-set: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <target> --onto <work-tip>`, then `jj new` to re-seat `@`; state/resume/dry-run/rollback + integration test
- **0.72.0-3** N-ochid stamping — commit-bot emits one `ochid:` per work commit the push newly publishes (the AGENTS.md merge-close-out shape; push today stamps only the topmost)
- **0.72.0-4** teach validate-desc + the push sanity verifiers the merge shape (two parents, N ochids)
- **0.72.0-5** docs (cycle-protocol recipe → wrapper flag, plus the codification folded from Ideas 1)
- **0.72.0** close-out — dogfooded by landing this cycle via `push --merge`

Key design decision worth your eye: the merge stage runs *after* both commits, so ochids are injected by the normal 1:1 stages and survive the rebase (chids are rebase-stable) — no trailer rewriting anywhere. Two things left open in the block: the flag name (`--merge`) and whether the ochid list skips commits already covered by interim 1:1 pushes (decided at the stamping step; `squash_push::extract_ochids` already gives a cheap coverage scan).

## Triage of the four entries

- **Todo 1 (pause point)** — retired unimplemented, absorbed into the cycle. Native merge support supersedes the pause; its companion prereq ("pin the bot repo to main") already landed at 0.68.0.
- **Todo 2 (refactor program)** — stays (now #1); annotated that split-push.rs and trapezoid close-out are pulled forward, with the in-process form folding into jj-lib migration. Matching status notes added to both stage sections in `refactor-20260716.md`.
- **Todo 8 (N:1 uncovered commits)** — stays (now #7), narrowed to the pure no-bot-pairings interop case: its "also covers a held-local cycle" claim moved into this cycle (that's exactly what the N-ochid merge stamping handles), and the stamping step is noted as shared groundwork.
- **Ideas 1 (codify ochid invariant …)** — retired: the still-live codification (ochid invariant, bot-repo rules, Ideas-aware Preparation/Close-out wording) folds into the 0.72.0-5 docs step; the squash-gating bullet was stale (merge non-ff has been the default since 0.64.0/0.67.1); the cross-repo migration sketch moved to `todo-backlog.md` as entry 48.

`fix-todo` renumbered (12 entries, validates clean), Ideas renumbered manually since fix-todo doesn't cover that section, and one now-stale "Todo #11" cross-reference was converted to a title reference.

Version bump to 0.72.0-0 and opening the chores section are the Preparation commit itself, so I've left them for after you review — the plan block in TODO.md is the thing to look at. If the ladder reads right (especially the split-push.rs step and the flag name), I'll run the 0.72.0-0 prep commit next.

message.content:
Reasonable, I want to be sure we in 100% agreement what a trapezoidal commit is,
it's a commit that has two parents one which points back to the parent of the
first commit in the task and the other which points to the penultimate commit
in the task. I might not be using the correct terminology so let's be sure
we agree.

Also, let's talk about the user experience. I'm thinking multi-step tasks
will be typically done on a branch through and including close-out and allowing it to
be thoroughly vetted before merging into main.

But there will be times when a change starts out simple and on main and then evolves
into a multi-step commit with all the work on main. This second style ends up common
on a repo that is only used by the author.  But I also want the "same" workflow to
be viable. I.e. work on a "branch" main or otherwise and then close-out. And then
decide if a trapezoidal commit is appropriate.

I think in the second case, working on main, it may require a "force" push if the
user doesn't want to add another commit, but if I'm correct the user should be able
to make it a trapezoidal comment either way.

On the other hand I'm hoping that working a non-main branch, the operation post
close-out commit that forms the trapezoidal commit never has to be a "force" push.

Thoughts only.

content.text:
Good discussion to have before -2 lands. Taking the two topics in turn — no file edits, thoughts only.

## Terminology check: yes, we agree

Your definition matches mine exactly, with one wording note. Counting the close-out itself as a task commit, the task is `-0, -1, …, -N, close-out`, and the trapezoid is:

```
◆    X.Y.Z close-out (the merge)
├─╮
│ ◆  X.Y.Z-N   ← second parent: penultimate commit of the task
│ ◆  X.Y.Z-1
│ ◆  X.Y.Z-0
├─╯
◆    previous close-out  ← first parent: parent of the task's first commit
```

- First parent: the parent of the task's first commit (the pre-task base — normally the previous cycle's close-out).
- Second parent: the last *Work* commit, which is the penultimate commit of the task because the close-out itself is the task's final commit.

One nuance worth keeping in mind: "parent of the first commit" and "current `main` tip" are the same commit only in a single-author repo where `main` didn't move during the task. If `main` had advanced (another contributor), a `git merge --no-ff` would use the *new* main tip as first parent and conflicts become possible. The trapezoid is the degenerate, conflict-free case — base is an ancestor of the tip — and push's preflight (`main == main@origin`) plus the planned ancestor precondition enforce exactly that case. So within vc-x1's world the two definitions coincide by construction.

## UX: both styles work, and your force-push intuitions are almost exactly right

**Branch style (work on a bookmark, close-out, vet, then merge):** no force push, ever, on `main`. The merge's first parent *is* the current `main` tip, so `main` only moves forward — an ordinary push. Even the vetting window costs nothing: if the ladder (and even a single-parent close-out) was pushed to the branch for review, forming the merge afterward rewrites at most the close-out commit; `main` still moves forward normally, and the now-stale branch ref just gets *deleted* — its commits stay reachable through the merge's second parent, so nothing is lost and nothing is force-updated. Your hope holds.

**Main style (task grows on `main`, everything already published):** here's the pleasant surprise — the trapezoid needs **no force push either**, as long as the close-out is the merge. The merge's *second* parent is `main`'s current tip, so the new `main` is a descendant of the old `main` — an ordinary fast-forward-style push. The work commits keep their published SHAs; the merge merely reroutes the *first-parent* line around them, so `git log --first-parent` reads one-commit-per-cycle even though the ladder rode on `main`. History stays append-only.

The only force case, in either style, is the **post-hoc conversion**: a single-parent close-out *already published on the target* that you then rewrite into a merge — that's your "doesn't want to add another commit" case, and it's exactly cycle-protocol's existing post-hoc caveat (`--ignore-immutable` + force-update). So the decision rule is simply: choose the shape *before* publishing the close-out and you never force; change your mind after, and you're rewriting published history.

**One real plan consequence your main-style scenario surfaces.** My drafted mechanics said "first parent = the target bookmark's current position." That's correct in branch style (`main` sits at the base) but wrong in main style — there `main` sits at the work *tip*, and using it as first parent would degenerate the merge. Push can't infer where the task started (nothing in the repo marks the ladder's first commit). So `--merge` needs an optional base: default to the target bookmark's position when it's a proper ancestor of the work tip (branch style), and require an explicit base revision when the bookmark is at the tip (main style) — e.g. `--merge` vs `--merge <base>`. I'd fold that into the 0.72.0-2 design bullets once we're done discussing.

The plain version: we mean the same thing by trapezoid — a close-out with two parents, one reaching back to just before the task began, one to the last piece of work. Both of your working styles can end in that shape, and neither needs a forced push if you pick the shape when you close out; forcing only enters if you publish a normal close-out first and reshape it afterward. The one adjustment to my plan: when the whole task lived on main, push can't guess where the task started, so the merge flag needs to accept a starting point.

On one hand you say when doing the "close=out" deciding on if it's trapezoidal
or not never needs a force push. But on the other hand I'd like that to be the
case when the "close-out" of completed on the branch and then it goes through
a vetting/review/pre-release phase. Like what would happens if a user doesn't
have commit rights but is working on a fork and then ask for a pull request (PR)
by the owners of the main repo.

How would something like that work? Thoughts only!

On one hand you say when doing the "close=out" deciding if it's trapezoidal
or not never needs a force push. But on the other hand I'd like that to be the
case when the "close-out" is completed on the branch and then it goes through
a vetting/review/pre-release phase.

This is what would happens if a user doesn't have commit rights but is working
on a fork and then creates a pull request (PR) on the primary repo and then
the owners would pull the change into the main or other branch. And eventually
it would migrate into a release branch.

How would something like that work? Thoughts only!

content.text:
This scenario actually resolves the tension you're pointing at, because it splits a thing my plan had fused: **who authors the merge**. Thoughts only.

## The PR flow produces the trapezoid — but the integrator authors it

In the fork/PR world the close-out cannot be the merge commit: the contributor doesn't have rights to `main`, and `main`'s tip at merge time isn't even knowable while the PR is under review. So the shape comes out like this:

```
◆    merge commit — authored by the maintainer     } primary repo
├─╮
│ ◆  X.Y.Z close-out      ← branch tip = PR head   }
│ ◆  X.Y.Z-N                                       } contributor's
│ ◆  X.Y.Z-1                                       } fork branch
├─╯
◆    main at merge time
```

The contributor's close-out stays a normal single-parent commit at the branch tip, exactly as you want: finished, pushed, vetted through the whole PR/pre-release phase, and **never rewritten**. The trapezoid's top edge is a *new* commit the maintainer creates (`git merge --no-ff`, or GitHub's "create a merge commit" button). Every step in the chain is append-only:

- contributor pushes branch to fork — forward push
- maintainer merges into `main` — new commit, forward
- `main` migrates into a release branch by merge — forward again

No force push anywhere, by construction. The only force in the whole picture is if review demands rework and the contributor amends their own fork branch — which is their private ref, and PRs absorb that routinely.

So there are two legitimate trapezoid variants, and the difference is ownership:

1. **Committer/solo variant** (what `push --merge` implements): close-out and merge are the *same* commit. Possible only because you own the target and its tip is fixed at close-out time. Elegant — no thin integration commit — but the price is exactly what you noticed: if the single-parent close-out was already published on the branch, forming the merge rewrites it.
2. **PR variant**: close-out and merge are *separate* commits. The close-out is immutable from the moment it's pushed for review; the merge belongs to the integrator. This variant gives you the "vetted close-out is never touched" guarantee unconditionally — and it's available solo, too: even in your own repo you can keep the close-out at the branch tip and create a distinct merge commit, at the cost of one extra commit whose message is a thin integration record (and the first-parent line then shows the merge's title, not the close-out's — the GitHub-boilerplate-message problem).

## The part that bites: ochids across the PR boundary

The merge button preserves the contributor's commits byte-for-byte, so jj's change IDs ride along and every `ochid:` keeps resolving in the primary repo — the provenance chain survives upstream intact. But GitHub's other two buttons — squash-merge and rebase-merge — mint *new* commits on the primary side: new SHAs, no jj change-id header, so new chids, and every ochid pointing into that task breaks. That is precisely the "ochids change at every merge until the change reaches the canonical repo's `main`" situation from the retired Ideas entry — backlog #48's cross-repo migration sketch — and your scenario is its motivating case. The practical rule for a repo adopting this workflow: integrate by merge commit, never squash/rebase, or accept that the bot-side links need a migration story.

## What this means for the 0.72.0 plan

Nothing structural — it sharpens the framing. `--merge` is the committer-owns-the-target shape; the PR flow needs no new vc-x1 machinery on the contributor side (a branch push with the close-out at its tip is already ordinary `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`). Two things worth a design bullet when we resume: state explicitly in the docs that the two variants exist and when each applies, and keep the door open for a future integrator-side helper (form the merge with a real message instead of forge boilerplate). Both are notes, not ladder changes.

The plain version: in the pull-request world, the reviewed work is never touched — the project owner adds one new "joining" commit on top, which is what makes the trapezoid there. So nobody ever has to force-push. Your solo workflow squeezes the joining commit and the close-out into one, which is tidier but only safe to reshape before it's published. The one real danger in the PR world is different: if the owner uses squash or rebase instead of a true merge, the commits get recreated and the links between your code history and session history snap — that's the migration problem we parked in the backlog.

aiTitle: Implement non-ff merge and trapezoidal close-out

content.content:
123	## Multi-user collaboration
124	
125	The bot thinks the same model — linear forest on the bot
126	side, multi-trailer reconciliation on the code side —
127	generalizes naturally to multi-user.
128	
129	### Bot repo as a forest of linear chains
130	
131	Each session is one append-only chain. The bot repo is a
132	forest of those chains; never merged. No bot-side merge
133	commits at all. Code-side has a DAG (merges, branches);
134	bot-side is a forest. The mapping is the multi-ochid trailer
135	set on each code commit, fanning out to the contributing
136	chain tips.
137	
138	The bot thinks this is cleaner than "bot side mirrors code
139	side topology" because:
140	
141	- Each conversation is a wire-format record of what was
142	  actually said. Merging two conversations into one commit
143	  would falsify that record.
144	- "Never rewrite bot commits" becomes an enforceable rule —
145	  change_ids on the bot side are automatically stable.
146	- Storage and sync stay simple — appending only, no merge
147	  conflict resolution on the bot side ever.
148	
149	### Multi-line ochid trailer
150	
151	Standard git trailer convention allows multiple values for
152	the same key. Just emit `ochid:` once per pointer:
153	
154	```
155	ochid: /.claude/abc123
156	ochid: /.claude/def456
157	ochid: /.claude/ghi789
158	```
159	
160	Single-pointer commits stay backward compatible — they're
161	just the N=1 case. No version bump needed; existing parsers
162	either already accept repeated trailers or are easy to
163	extend.
164	
165	### When multi-ochid actually applies
166	
167	- **Code-side merge with no extra bot work** — N ochids =
168	  the merge parents' contributing tip change_ids (one per
169	  parent). Possibly with de-duplication if parents share
170	  ancestry.
171	- **Code-side merge with conflict-resolution session** —
172	  N parents' ochids + the resolution session's tip ochid.
173	  If user A and user B merge into M and user C resolves the
174	  conflict, M has 3 ochids: A's tip, B's tip, C's session
175	  tip.
176	- **Single-user single-session** — still 1 ochid. Same as
177	  today.
178	- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
179	  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
180	  as single-user.
181	
182	### Staged fork integration
183	
184	How a contributed cycle's worth of changes enters a
185	downstream repo, keeping the as-is record separate from the
186	integration work:
187	
188	- **As-is acceptance.** A contributed set of changes lands
189	  first in a dedicated branch *exactly as submitted* — no
190	  merge, no squash, no tweak. The contributor's `ochid:`
191	  trailers are preserved verbatim; the branch is a faithful
192	  record of what was contributed.
193	- **Integration as separate steps.** Merging, squashing, or
194	  tweaking that work onto the target line happens
195	  afterward, as one or more separate steps on an
196	  intermediate branch. Each is its own cycle and may add
197	  its own bot session.
198	- **Resulting ochid set.** An integrated commit therefore
199	  carries a *set* of ochids — the contributor's original
200	  pointer(s) plus any integration-session pointer(s): the
201	  [multi-line ochid trailer](#multi-line-ochid-trailer)
202	  applied to the fork case rather than the octopus-merge
203	  case.
204	
205	The contributing bot repos live in different locations
206	(different users, different remotes), so each ochid in the
207	set must name *which* bot repo as well as the chid. Three
208	representations, in increasing indirection:
209	
210	- **Workspace-relative path** — `/.claude/<chid>`,
211	  `/.claude-alice/<chid>`. Today's form; resolves only
212	  repos under the local workspace root.
213	- **Full URL** — `https://github.com/alice/bot#<chid>`
214	  (see [Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped
215	  ochid](#per-user-bot-repos-via-url-shaped-ochid)).
216	  Self-contained, no external table; verbose, exposed to
217	  link rot.
218	- **`.vc-config.toml` repo index** — a tuple
219	  `(repo-index, chid)` where `repo-index` keys a
220	  repo-location table declared once in `.vc-config.toml`.
221	  The trailer stays short, and relocating a repo is a
222	  one-line config edit instead of rewriting every trailer.
223	  Cost: indirection — the trailer is meaningless without
224	  the config, so a commit inspected outside its workspace
225	  (a raw `git show` elsewhere) can't resolve it.
226	
227	The bot thinks index and URL forms are complementary, not
228	rivals — index for repos the project already tracks in
229	`.vc-config.toml`, URL for one-off external contributors —
230	and a resolver accepting all three (path, URL, index) is
231	the one-rule dispatch the URL-shaped section already
232	describes, with a third arm.
233	
234	### Per-user vs shared bot repo
235	
236	Open question worth deciding before code lands.
237	
238	- **Shared bot repo** (today's model extended): one
239	  `.claude/` repo holds all sessions across all users. Push
240	  from any user appends a chain. Pros: viewer sees
241	  everything in one place; cross-references resolve locally.
242	  Cons: every user pulls every other user's session data on
243	  sync — could be heavy. Permissions are coarse.
244	- **Per-user bot repos**: each user has their own
245	  `.claude-<user>/` repo, ochid trailers carry a user
246	  qualifier (`ochid: /.claude-alice/abc123`). Pros:
247	  lightweight sync, natural permissions. Cons: viewer needs
248	  to know all the bot-repo URLs to show full context;
249	  offline viewing breaks if you don't have the relevant
250	  user's repo.
251	
252	The bot thinks **shared** is the default with a per-project
253	escape hatch — most teams want the cross-references to
254	"just work." The qualifier in ochid (the path component
255	after the leading `/`) already encodes either model:
256	`/.claude/<chid>` for shared, `/.claude-alice/<chid>` for
257	per-user. The trailer format itself is forward-compatible.
258	
259	### Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped ochid
260	
261	Per-user bot repos become practical for distributed
262	projects (open-source, multi-org) when ochid trailers can
263	qualify by URL rather than only workspace-relative path.
264	This generalizes the per-user model in the section above
265	from "co-located teams sharing a workspace" to "anyone on
266	the public internet."
267	
268	**Trailer shape.** Existing path form is
269	workspace-root-relative (`/.claude/<chid>` for shared,
270	`/.claude-alice/<chid>` for per-user-in-shared-workspace).
271	URL form generalizes the qualifier to a remote repo:
272	
273	```
274	ochid: https://github.com/alice/bot-sessions#<chid>
275	ochid: git@github.com:alice/bot-sessions.git#<chid>
276	```
277	
278	Parser dispatch is one rule: if the qualifier parses as a
279	URL, fetch over the network; otherwise resolve relative
280	to workspace root. Existing path-form trailers stay the
281	N=1 backward-compatible case. No schema versioning
282	needed.
283	
284	**Link rot is the load-bearing problem.** A contributor
285	can publish their bot repo, get merged, then later make
286	it private or delete it. The ochid URL 404s; bot context
287	is lost. The bot thinks this is a real-but-not-
288	catastrophic leak — the commit's code and
289	`Signed-off-by:` line remain authoritative; bot context
290	is a nice-to-have for understanding patch development,
291	not a load-bearing semantic. Direct analogue: dead URLs
292	in academic citations, mailing-list links in older
293	kernel commit messages. Annoying for archaeology, not
294	fatal.
295	
296	**Mitigations, in order of practical adoption:**
297	
298	1. **Project-side mirroring at merge time.** When a
299	   maintainer accepts a PR, project tooling fetches the
300	   contributor's bot repo at the referenced chid and
301	   mirrors to a project-namespace archive (e.g.
302	   `bot-archives/<contributor>-<date>-<chid>.jsonl`).

file.content:
## Multi-user collaboration

The bot thinks the same model — linear forest on the bot
side, multi-trailer reconciliation on the code side —
generalizes naturally to multi-user.

### Bot repo as a forest of linear chains

Each session is one append-only chain. The bot repo is a
forest of those chains; never merged. No bot-side merge
commits at all. Code-side has a DAG (merges, branches);
bot-side is a forest. The mapping is the multi-ochid trailer
set on each code commit, fanning out to the contributing
chain tips.

The bot thinks this is cleaner than "bot side mirrors code
side topology" because:

- Each conversation is a wire-format record of what was
  actually said. Merging two conversations into one commit
  would falsify that record.
- "Never rewrite bot commits" becomes an enforceable rule —
  change_ids on the bot side are automatically stable.
- Storage and sync stay simple — appending only, no merge
  conflict resolution on the bot side ever.

### Multi-line ochid trailer

Standard git trailer convention allows multiple values for
the same key. Just emit `ochid:` once per pointer:

```
ochid: /.claude/abc123
ochid: /.claude/def456
ochid: /.claude/ghi789
```

Single-pointer commits stay backward compatible — they're
just the N=1 case. No version bump needed; existing parsers
either already accept repeated trailers or are easy to
extend.

### When multi-ochid actually applies

- **Code-side merge with no extra bot work** — N ochids =
  the merge parents' contributing tip change_ids (one per
  parent). Possibly with de-duplication if parents share
  ancestry.
- **Code-side merge with conflict-resolution session** —
  N parents' ochids + the resolution session's tip ochid.
  If user A and user B merge into M and user C resolves the
  conflict, M has 3 ochids: A's tip, B's tip, C's session
  tip.
- **Single-user single-session** — still 1 ochid. Same as
  today.
- **Code rebase** — ochids unchanged (change_ids are
  stable). Same as today. Multi-user rebases work the same
  as single-user.

### Staged fork integration

How a contributed cycle's worth of changes enters a
downstream repo, keeping the as-is record separate from the
integration work:

- **As-is acceptance.** A contributed set of changes lands
  first in a dedicated branch *exactly as submitted* — no
  merge, no squash, no tweak. The contributor's `ochid:`
  trailers are preserved verbatim; the branch is a faithful
  record of what was contributed.
- **Integration as separate steps.** Merging, squashing, or
  tweaking that work onto the target line happens
  afterward, as one or more separate steps on an
  intermediate branch. Each is its own cycle and may add
  its own bot session.
- **Resulting ochid set.** An integrated commit therefore
  carries a *set* of ochids — the contributor's original
  pointer(s) plus any integration-session pointer(s): the
  [multi-line ochid trailer](#multi-line-ochid-trailer)
  applied to the fork case rather than the octopus-merge
  case.

The contributing bot repos live in different locations
(different users, different remotes), so each ochid in the
set must name *which* bot repo as well as the chid. Three
representations, in increasing indirection:

- **Workspace-relative path** — `/.claude/<chid>`,
  `/.claude-alice/<chid>`. Today's form; resolves only
  repos under the local workspace root.
- **Full URL** — `https://github.com/alice/bot#<chid>`
  (see [Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped
  ochid](#per-user-bot-repos-via-url-shaped-ochid)).
  Self-contained, no external table; verbose, exposed to
  link rot.
- **`.vc-config.toml` repo index** — a tuple
  `(repo-index, chid)` where `repo-index` keys a
  repo-location table declared once in `.vc-config.toml`.
  The trailer stays short, and relocating a repo is a
  one-line config edit instead of rewriting every trailer.
  Cost: indirection — the trailer is meaningless without
  the config, so a commit inspected outside its workspace
  (a raw `git show` elsewhere) can't resolve it.

The bot thinks index and URL forms are complementary, not
rivals — index for repos the project already tracks in
`.vc-config.toml`, URL for one-off external contributors —
and a resolver accepting all three (path, URL, index) is
the one-rule dispatch the URL-shaped section already
describes, with a third arm.

### Per-user vs shared bot repo

Open question worth deciding before code lands.

- **Shared bot repo** (today's model extended): one
  `.claude/` repo holds all sessions across all users. Push
  from any user appends a chain. Pros: viewer sees
  everything in one place; cross-references resolve locally.
  Cons: every user pulls every other user's session data on
  sync — could be heavy. Permissions are coarse.
- **Per-user bot repos**: each user has their own
  `.claude-<user>/` repo, ochid trailers carry a user
  qualifier (`ochid: /.claude-alice/abc123`). Pros:
  lightweight sync, natural permissions. Cons: viewer needs
  to know all the bot-repo URLs to show full context;
  offline viewing breaks if you don't have the relevant
  user's repo.

The bot thinks **shared** is the default with a per-project
escape hatch — most teams want the cross-references to
"just work." The qualifier in ochid (the path component
after the leading `/`) already encodes either model:
`/.claude/<chid>` for shared, `/.claude-alice/<chid>` for
per-user. The trailer format itself is forward-compatible.

### Per-user bot repos via URL-shaped ochid

Per-user bot repos become practical for distributed
projects (open-source, multi-org) when ochid trailers can
qualify by URL rather than only workspace-relative path.
This generalizes the per-user model in the section above
from "co-located teams sharing a workspace" to "anyone on
the public internet."

**Trailer shape.** Existing path form is
workspace-root-relative (`/.claude/<chid>` for shared,
`/.claude-alice/<chid>` for per-user-in-shared-workspace).
URL form generalizes the qualifier to a remote repo:

```
ochid: https://github.com/alice/bot-sessions#<chid>
ochid: git@github.com:alice/bot-sessions.git#<chid>
```

Parser dispatch is one rule: if the qualifier parses as a
URL, fetch over the network; otherwise resolve relative
to workspace root. Existing path-form trailers stay the
N=1 backward-compatible case. No schema versioning
needed.

**Link rot is the load-bearing problem.** A contributor
can publish their bot repo, get merged, then later make
it private or delete it. The ochid URL 404s; bot context
is lost. The bot thinks this is a real-but-not-
catastrophic leak — the commit's code and
`Signed-off-by:` line remain authoritative; bot context
is a nice-to-have for understanding patch development,
not a load-bearing semantic. Direct analogue: dead URLs
in academic citations, mailing-list links in older
kernel commit messages. Annoying for archaeology, not
fatal.

**Mitigations, in order of practical adoption:**

1. **Project-side mirroring at merge time.** When a
   maintainer accepts a PR, project tooling fetches the
   contributor's bot repo at the referenced chid and
   mirrors to a project-namespace archive (e.g.
   `bot-archives/<contributor>-<date>-<chid>.jsonl`).

content.text:
One correction first, then the good news: your dream has a design doc already — `notes/forks-multi-user.md` (it's the `[[10]]`-style ref behind the N:1 Todo) anticipates most of this, and in a couple places lands somewhere better than the framing in your message.

## Correction: a true non-ff merge does *not* change chids

That's the whole reason to insist on it. The merge button preserves your commits byte-for-byte — same SHAs, same embedded change IDs — and only *adds* the integrator's merge commit on top. So your ochids don't merely "survive in the commit bodies awaiting repair": they keep **resolving directly**, no migration step, in every clone (per the resolvability note in AGENTS.md — the chid travels with a pushed commit). Chids change only under squash-merge or rebase-merge, which mint new commits; that's the only case where backlog #48's migration story is needed. So under merge-commit integration, the future viewer genuinely "just works" with zero repair.

## Other people's bot repos: the doc says it *can* work — without merging them

Two points where forks-multi-user.md refines your framing:

- **"If they have their own bot repo, it doesn't work" — actually it can.** The fix is in the ochid qualifier: `/.claude/<chid>` assumes *the* companion, but the doc generalizes the prefix to name *which* bot repo — `/.claude-alice/<chid>` for per-user repos in a shared workspace, or a URL form (`ochid: https://github.com/alice/bot-sessions#<chid>`) for arbitrary contributors. Chids are effectively globally unique, so resolution across a set of bot repos is well-defined; the viewer just needs access to (or a mirror of) the referenced repo. Link rot is the acknowledged weak point, with project-side mirroring at merge time as the leading mitigation.

- **Bot repos are never merged — deliberately.** The doc's model is the bot repo as a *forest of append-only linear chains*, one chain per session, with no bot-side merge commits ever: a conversation is a wire-format record of what was actually said, and merging two conversations into one commit would falsify that record. The cross-user joining happens on the *code* side instead — the multi-line ochid trailer fans out from a code commit to all contributing chain tips (e.g. a conflict-resolution merge carrying three ochids: Alice's tip, Bob's tip, and the resolver's session). Your "PR for my bot repo" idea slots straight into this as the *shared bot repo* model: accepting a bot PR means appending your chains, never content-merging them. And your UUID observation is exactly why that's frictionless — claude-code's one-file-per-session UUID `.jsonl` naming makes any two contributors' session sets file-disjoint, so even a naive git-level merge of bot PRs is trivially clean. Collision risk is, as you say, minuscule.

The paired-PR flow then has one ordering rule, which is just the ochid invariant wearing a new hat: every public ochid must resolve in the public graph, so the bot-repo PR should land with (or before) the work-repo PR, and *both* sides need commit-preserving integration — a squash on either side breaks the links in that direction.

## On agent-company cooperation

The realistic bar is the one you set at the end: **non-collision, not a shared format**. Distinct agents writing distinct files in distinct directories can coexist in one bot repo indefinitely; what needs per-agent work is the *viewer*, and vc-x1's existing bot-session design — the two-layer tolerant parse that keeps the raw record and models what it recognizes, with `transcript-format.md` as the claude-code adapter's SSOT — is already the right shape for that: adapters per format, graceful degradation for the unknown. A universal agreed format would be nice; it isn't load-bearing.

The plain version: you're better off than you thought — a proper merge keeps your commit IDs intact, so nothing ever needs fixing. Strangers' conversations can be linked too, as long as each link says whose conversation repo it points into. And nobody's conversations ever get blended together: conversation history only ever grows by appending, and it's the code commits that point outward to everyone who contributed. The whole scheme needs agents to avoid stepping on each other's files, not to agree on one format — and one-file-per-conversation with unique names, which claude-code already does, is enough.

aiTitle: Implement non-ff merge and trapezoidal close-out

content.text:
You have one wire crossed: **a non-ff merge is not a rebase**. Three different operations, and only one of them rewrites anything:

**Fast-forward merge — slide the pointer, add nothing.** Your intuition here is right: it produces exactly the history you'd get by working directly on main. The branch's commits already sit ahead of main's tip, so git just moves the `main` pointer forward along them. No new commit, nothing rewritten, and afterward the branch leaves no trace in the graph — you can't tell a branch ever existed.

```
before:                after ff:
  ◆ C  ← topic           ◆ C  ← main (pointer moved)
  ◆ B                    ◆ B
  ◆ A  ← main            ◆ A
```

**Non-ff merge — add one new commit, rewrite nothing.** `--no-ff` means "even though you *could* fast-forward, don't" — instead create a real merge commit `M` with two parents. `A`, `B`, `C` keep their exact SHAs (and chids); `M` is the only new object. This is pure addition, and `M` *is* the trapezoid commit — first parent `A` (the pre-task base), second parent `C` (the last work commit):

```
  ◆ M  ← main (new commit, parents A + C)
  ├─╮
  │ ◆ C   ← untouched
  │ ◆ B   ← untouched
  ├─╯
  ◆ A
```

**Rebase — copy commits, SHAs change.** This is the only operation of the three that rewrites: `B`,`C` are re-created as `B'`,`C'` on a new base. When *jj* does the rebase locally, it carries the change ID through the rewrite — that's precisely why our close-out flow can rebase the close-out commit into the merge shape and every ochid stays valid: new SHA, same chid. But when *GitHub's server* does it (the "Rebase and merge" or "Squash and merge" buttons), GitHub mints fresh commits with no knowledge of jj — the change-id doesn't come along, so there even the chids break. That's the case backlog #48 exists for.

So the mapping to our discussion: the trapezoid is a **non-ff merge commit**, and nothing about creating one changes any SHA. The solo `push --merge` flow uses one *local jj rebase* purely to shape the not-yet-published close-out into that merge (chid-safe, and nothing published gets touched); the PR flow needs no rebase at all — the maintainer's merge button creates `M` directly.

The plain version: fast-forward adds nothing and just moves the label; a non-ff merge adds exactly one new "joining" commit and touches nothing else; rebase is the odd one out — it re-creates commits, which changes their git identities. Your work survives a proper merge untouched. The confusion probably came from our close-out recipe using the word "rebase" — there it's only a local shaping step on a commit nobody has seen yet.

message.content: But there could be merge conflicits what happens then?

content.text:
Short answer: in *our* trapezoid, conflicts are impossible by construction; conflicts only enter when the two parents have **diverged**, and then the resolution lives in the merge commit itself — still without rewriting anybody's work.

**The solo close-out can't conflict.** A content conflict needs two sides that each changed something since their common ancestor. In the trapezoid, the first parent (the pre-task base) *is* an ancestor of the second parent (the work tip) — only one side moved. There's nothing to reconcile: the merge commit's content is simply the close-out's content, and the two-parent structure is pure bookkeeping. That's why the refactor doc calls the merge trivial ("no conflicts possible since the base is an ancestor of the tip"), and it's exactly what the planned `--merge` precondition enforces — if the target has moved and isn't an ancestor of the tip anymore, `push --merge` should refuse rather than attempt a real content merge.

**When parents diverge (the PR world), it's a real merge.** If `main` advanced while your task was in review and both sides touched the same lines, the non-ff merge has genuine work to do. The key point for your provenance concerns: the resolution is **new content recorded in the merge commit `M`** — your branch commits are still untouched, SHAs and chids intact. Nothing about conflicts forces a rewrite. The forge just won't do it silently; GitHub greys out the button ("this branch has conflicts"), and there are three standard ways forward:

1. **Merge main into the branch** (contributor side): pull main into your topic branch, resolve there, push. Append-only — no force push, no rewrites; the PR then merges clean. The cost is an extra merge commit inside the branch history.
2. **Rebase the branch onto new main** (contributor side): re-creates your commits on the new base — force-push, but only to your own fork, and if jj does the rebase your chids (and thus ochids) survive the rewrite.
3. **Maintainer resolves at merge time**: they do the merge locally, fix the conflicts, and commit — the resolution is theirs, inside `M`.

**The dual-repo story already accounts for this.** Conflict resolution is real intellectual work, which means it's a *session* — and forks-multi-user.md handles it with the multi-ochid trailer: if Alice's and Bob's work merge into `M` and Carol resolves the conflict, `M` carries three ochids — Alice's chain tip, Bob's, and Carol's resolution session. The "why did the merge choose this?" conversation is preserved just like any other work.

One jj-specific aside: jj is unusually relaxed here — it can *commit a conflict unresolved* (conflicts are first-class objects in commits) and let you resolve later. That's handy for manual surgery, but not something `push --merge` should lean on; refusing diverged parents is the right behavior for the tool.

The plain version: your close-out merge can never conflict, because only your side changed anything. Conflicts appear only when the main line moved underneath you — then someone has to reconcile the two sides, and the fix is written into the new joining commit, not into your history. Whoever reconciles it does that work in a conversation of their own, and the joining commit can point to that conversation too.

Your statement single user "close-out merge can never conflict" is false, I could be working on
two instances of the repo on the same or different computers and conflicts could

Ok so we can have a forest of bot repos, but there there needs to be a link to the associated

content.text:
Both points land — the first is a genuine correction, the second is right and worth stating precisely.

## Conceded: "single user" was the wrong condition

You're right. The guarantee never came from user-count — it comes from topology: *the base must still be an ancestor of the tip at merge time*. Two instances of the same repo (same person, two machines, or even two working copies) are two writers; if instance B pushed to `main` while instance A's task was in flight, A's base is no longer the `main` tip and A is in exactly the diverged case, regardless of it being one human.

The correct statement is: **the merge step itself never conflicts, because push refuses to run it when the parents have diverged.** The preflight (`sync --check`, `main == main@origin`) and the planned ancestor precondition detect the moved `main` before anything is committed, and the reconciliation happens as its own prior step — sync, then either merge the new `main` into your work or rebase your ladder onto it — and *that* step is where conflicts can appear. So conflicts aren't impossible for a solo user; they're hoisted out of the close-out merge into an explicit, earlier operation. Same remedies as the PR case, because it *is* the PR case with yourself as the other contributor.

## The links: yes — and here's precisely what "survive" requires

The link you're describing already exists and is bidirectional, living in the commit *messages* (trailers) on both sides:

- **code → bot**: the work commit's body carries `ochid: /.claude/<botchid>` — which session produced this change.
- **bot → code**: the bot commit's body carries `ochid: /<workchid>` — which work commit(s) this session produced.

A trailer is part of the commit object, so each *link record* survives anything that preserves its commit. The fragile part is never the record — it's the **target**: each link points at a chid, so a link survives exactly when the commit it points at keeps its chid. That gives a clean survival table for integration events:

- **Non-ff merge**: both directions survive outright. Neither side's commits are touched; the new merge commit `M` is pure addition (and can carry its own ochids — the contributors' chain tips plus an integration/resolution session, per the multi-ochid design).
- **jj rebase** (local shaping, or reconciling your other instance's push): survives — new SHAs, same chids, and chids are what ochids point at. This is the load-bearing reason the whole scheme uses chids rather than SHAs.
- **Squash / forge-side rebase**: the code commits are re-minted with new chids, so every **bot → code** link now points at chids that exist only in the pre-integration branch. The records themselves are intact in the bot chains — nothing on the bot side is ever rewritten — they've just gone stale. The repair is append-only too: a migration record mapping old chid → landed chid (backlog #48's sketch), never editing history.

The governing rule from the retired Ideas entry ties it together: *every public ochid must resolve in the public graph*. "Links must survive a merge" is that invariant applied at integration time — and non-ff merge is the one integration mode that upholds it with no extra machinery.

The plain version: I was wrong to say "one person can't conflict" — one person with two checkouts is two writers, and the tool's real protection is that it checks whether the main line moved and makes you reconcile *first*, rather than conflicting mid-merge. On your second point: yes — code history and conversation history each carry a pointer to the other, written into the commit text. Those pointers name the partner's permanent jj identity, so a true merge never breaks them; only squash-style integration re-mints identities, and the fix for that is adding a forwarding note, never rewriting anything.

aiTitle: Implement non-ff merge and trapezoidal close-out

content.text:
Recorded. It's now Todo #2 — "**ochid: bot-repo location qualifier**" — right behind the refactor program, with the list renumbered and validating clean (13 entries). The entry points at the forks-multi-user.md section you linked and notes your bot repo's actual home (`github.com/winksaville/vc-x1.claude`, currently discoverable only by convention).

Two things I baked into the entry worth knowing:

- **A cheap first rung short of URL trailers**: declare the companion's URL once in the committed `.vc-config.toml`. That closes the immediate gap you described — anyone cloning just the work repo can find the bot repo — with zero trailer-format change, and it rides naturally with the refactor program's facade-owns-topology stage, which is already making bot-repo location a config concern.
- The full solution (URL-shaped trailers + the config repo-index form, one-rule resolver dispatch, existing `/.claude/<chid>` trailers as the backward-compatible case) stays as sketched in the doc; nothing needs re-deciding when you pick it up.

So the queue reads: finish the merge close-out cycle (plan's in `## In Progress`, awaiting your review before the 0.72.0-0 prep commit), then this is sitting at #2 when you're ready for it.

content.text: Yes — the discussion surfaced one substantive design change and one docs-scope addition; squash and rebase turn out to need *no* ladder changes. Let me fold them into the `## In Progress` block.

input.old_string:
- Design points for the merge stage:
     - merge sits after commit-bot, before bookmark-set —
       chids survive the rebase, so both ochid directions
       are injected normally and stay valid
     - mechanics: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <target
       bookmark> --onto <work tip>` then `jj new <merge>`
       to re-seat an empty `@`
     - preconditions: target is an ancestor of the work
       tip; close-out has exactly one parent pre-rebase;
       degenerate single-commit cycle errors (plain push)
input.new_string:
- Design points for the merge stage:
     - merge sits after commit-bot, before bookmark-set —
       chids survive the rebase, so both ochid directions
       are injected normally and stay valid
     - `--merge [<base>]` — base is the merge's first
       parent (the parent of the task's first commit):
       - branch style (task on a feature bookmark, target
         bookmark sitting at the base): base defaults to
         the target bookmark's position
       - main style (task grew on the target, bookmark
         sitting at the work tip): base can't be inferred
         — the explicit `<base>` revision is required
     - mechanics: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <base>
       --onto <work tip>` then `jj new <merge>` to re-seat
       an empty `@`
     - preconditions: base is a proper ancestor of the
       work tip; close-out has exactly one parent
       pre-rebase; target bookmark is at the base or at
       the work tip — anything else is divergence (e.g.
       another repo instance moved the target): reconcile
       first, preflight's sync check catches the moved
       remote
     - both styles publish forward — the old bookmark
       position is one of the merge's parents, so the push
       never forces; post-hoc conversion of an
       already-published close-out stays the manual
       `--ignore-immutable` recipe (cycle-protocol's
       post-hoc caveat), not this flag

content.text:
Plan updated. Here's what changed and, just as importantly, what deliberately didn't:

**Changed — the `--merge` surface and its preconditions** (the one real gap the discussion exposed):

- The flag is now `--merge [<base>]`. In branch style the target bookmark sits at the base, so base defaults to the bookmark's position — no argument needed. In main style the bookmark sits at the work *tip*, so push can't infer where the task started and the explicit `<base>` revision is required.
- Preconditions reworked: base must be a proper ancestor of the work tip, and the target bookmark must be at either the base or the tip — any other position means divergence (your two-instances scenario) and push refuses, telling you to reconcile first; preflight's sync check already catches the moved remote.
- A new bullet pins down the force-push rule we converged on: both styles publish forward (the old bookmark position is always one of the merge's parents), so `--merge` never forces; post-hoc conversion of an already-published close-out explicitly stays the manual `--ignore-immutable` recipe, outside this flag.
- The 0.72.0-2 step now tests both styles (branch-based and on-target ladders).

**Changed — docs step scope**: 0.72.0-5 now also documents the two trapezoid variants (committer-owned, where the close-out *is* the merge — this flag — vs PR-style, where the integrator authors the merge and vc-x1 needs no machinery) and the chid-survival argument for merge-commit integration over squash/rebase, pointing at forks-multi-user.md.

**Unchanged, on purpose:**

- **Squashing** — pre-publication squash needs no tooling (just shape the ladder before pushing), which is why `push --squash` was demoted to the backlog at 0.67.1; nothing new here.
- **Local rebasing** — already the plan's core mechanism, and chid-safe by jj's design; no additions needed.
- **Forge-side squash/rebase breakage** — that's the cross-repo migration problem, parked as backlog #48, correctly out of this cycle.
- The ladder itself: still seven steps, same order — the changes are all within existing steps' design bullets.

The block validates clean. Ready for your review whenever — next move on your go-ahead is the 0.72.0-0 Preparation commit (version bump, chores section, this pickup).

message.content:
LG, back to ochid. if I clone an someone else's repo and I create a bot repo for my use and I
choose to add ochid's with URLs and the receiver is able to merge my commit without
any conflicts everything just works. There needs to be no ./vc-config.toml the
URL provides all that is needed. But when there is conflicts and resolved by the
owner all bets are off. But if they ask me to fix it and eventually the owner is able to merge
my PR without conflicts resolution the forest works.

content.text:
Mostly right, and the middle claim is actually *too pessimistic* — one correction there, plus a sharp edge on the "fix it" path.

**The clean case: yes, and it's better than "just works" — it's unilateral.** URL-shaped ochids make the scheme self-contained: the upstream repo needs no `.vc-config.toml`, no vc-x1, no awareness the scheme exists. Your commits carry `ochid: https://github.com/you/bot#<chid>` trailers as inert text; the owner merges them like any PR; anyone who later cares can follow the URL. Adoption is entirely on your side (your local workspace still has config so *your* tooling knows where your bot repo is — but nothing of that needs to reach upstream). The one dependency you take on is the URL staying alive — link rot is the doc's acknowledged weak point, with project-side mirroring as the mitigation for projects that come to care.

**The correction: owner-resolved conflicts do *not* void all bets.** Standard conflict resolution happens *inside the merge commit* — the owner reconciles the two sides and commits the result as `M`'s content. Your commits are still untouched: same SHAs, same chids, every ochid in both directions still resolves. What's actually lost is small and local: `M`'s content differs from your tip, and that resolution delta has no conversation attached (the owner presumably has no bot repo — in the full multi-user design that's exactly where `M` would carry the resolver's session ochid, but an owner not using the scheme just leaves `M` unattributed). Your forest survives intact; there's simply one commit at the seam whose "why" is only in the PR discussion.

All bets are genuinely off only when integration **re-mints your commits**: squash-merge, forge-side rebase-merge, or cherry-picking. Those change chids, and then the bot→code half of your links goes stale until a migration record repairs it.

**The "they ask me to fix it" path: right, with one trap.** How you fix it matters:

- **Merge main into your branch** and resolve there — append-only, nothing rewritten, safest.
- **Rebase with jj** — your commits are re-created but jj carries the change-ids through, so your bot repo's pointers still resolve. Force-push to your own fork, forest fine.
- **Rebase with plain git** — the trap. `git rebase` recreates commits *without* preserving the change-id header jj embeds, so you'd break your own links before the owner ever touches the PR. In this workflow, history surgery goes through jj, full stop.

Then the fix-up session itself is just a new chain appended to your bot repo, the reworked commits carry fresh trailers, and when the owner's eventual merge is conflict-free (or even conflict-resolved!), the whole record — original attempt, review feedback response, final shape — is preserved.

One symmetry worth noticing for later: the bot→code direction has the same locating problem in reverse — your bot commits say `ochid: /<chid>`, assuming "the" work repo. For your fork scenario context makes it obvious, but the URL qualifier generalizes to that direction too; something for the Todo #2 design pass, not for now.

The plain version: you can adopt this scheme alone — the other project needs nothing, your commits just carry web links to your conversation history. A maintainer fixing conflicts during a merge doesn't break anything; only the merge styles that *recreate* your commits (squash, rebase-button, cherry-pick) snap the links. And if you're ever asked to rework a PR, do the surgery with jj, not raw git rebase — jj preserves the identities the links point at; git doesn't.

content.content:
1	# Forking and multi-user coordination
2	
3	Captures design discussion about two related but separable
4	concerns:
5	
6	1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
7	   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
8	2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
9	   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.
10	
11	Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
12	the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
13	bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
14	conversations that should never be rewritten.
15	
16	For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
17	versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory
18	layout), see the companion file
19	[`bot-data-formats.md`](bot-data-formats.md).
20	
21	This file is forward-looking: most of what it describes is
22	the bot thinks, not implemented. Treat as a design reference,
23	not a status doc.
24	
25	## Forking with the bot side staying linear
26	
27	### The asymmetric situation
28	
29	When work needs to happen on an older code-side base — e.g.,
30	0.41.1 work branched off 0.41.0 while 0.42.0 is in flight on
31	main — the code repo can fork without difficulty. That's a
32	normal git/jj branch.
33	
34	The bot repo can't fork the same way: each session is a linear
35	conversation, and rewriting an existing session line would
36	falsify the historical record. The conversation that happened
37	is the conversation that happened; it doesn't get re-played
38	from a different branch base.
39	
40	### chid stability across rebase
41	
42	jj's two-id model is built for this scenario. Each commit has
43	both:
44	
45	- `commit_id` — git SHA, changes on rewrite.
46	- `change_id` — stable across `describe` / `rebase` /
47	  `squash-into-self`.
48	
49	`ochid:` trailers carry change_ids. So when a code-side branch
50	later rebases on top of another branch, change_ids are
51	preserved and ochid trailers in both directions still resolve.
52	
53	The bot thinks the only operations that break this are
54	`jj squash` (folding two commits into one — collapses two
55	change_ids into one) and `jj abandon` (removing a commit —
56	loses the change_id entirely). Pure rebase is safe.
57	
58	### Why not time-based linkers
59	
60	Time isn't a stronger primitive than change_id:
61	
62	- Clock skew between processes / repos breaks ordering.
63	- Time still doesn't survive rewrites any better than
64	  change_id does — you'd still need a stable identifier baked
65	  into the trailer that matches some property of the
66	  counterpart commit.
67	- change_id is jj's intended stability primitive — that's
68	  literally the job it exists to do.
69	
70	If the change_id scheme ever shows weakness, the bot thinks
71	the answer is jj-side hygiene (don't `squash`-collapse during
72	rebase) rather than switching primitives.
73	
74	### Partner bookmarks
75	
76	The chosen technique for forking the code side while keeping
77	the bot side linear: **partner bookmarks**.
78	
79	When a code-side branch needs to advance independently, create
80	a bookmark of the same name on the bot side starting at the
81	*current* bot HEAD — not at the historical bot commit paired
82	with the code-side branch base. The two bookmarks share a name
83	and advance together via `vc-x1 push`, but each carries its
84	own repo's state.
85	
86	Other names considered and rejected:
87	
88	- **mirror** — implies symmetric content. The bookmarks are
89	  not mirrors; they hold different content with no
90	  point-by-point correspondence.
91	- **tandem** — captures cooperation, but slightly less common
92	  in software vocabulary.
93	- **paired** — bland; "pair" can imply matching halves.
94	- **disjoint** / **asymmetric** — describe a property of the
95	  relationship, not a label for the technique.
96	- **yoked** — accurate metaphor but obscure.
97	
98	**partner** won because it conveys cooperation without
99	implying duplication. Always subject to change as we live
100	with the term.
101	
102	The asymmetric ordering between code and bot history (rebased
103	main commits on the code side will eventually point at bot
104	commits that come *before* their partner-branch pairs in bot
105	history) is a cosmetic wrinkle, not a correctness problem —
106	change_ids resolve regardless of position.
107	
108	### Recovery anchor
109	
110	The original main HEAD — the commit being "branched away
111	from" — should stay put, not be rewritten or rewound. It's
112	the recovery anchor for the operation. If the partner-branch
113	work goes wrong, main is still where it was, and recovery is
114	`jj abandon` on the new branch's commits.
115	
116	In the 0.41.1 init+clone refactor, this means main stays at
117	its current 0.42.0-4 docs-capture commit (`55eadc8`) for the
118	duration of the side-branch work. That commit becomes
119	redundant after the eventual rebase — its content has been
120	re-landed on `init-clone-refactor` — and gets dropped during
121	the rebase rather than being rewound now.
122

file.content:
# Forking and multi-user coordination

Captures design discussion about two related but separable
concerns:

1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.

Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
conversations that should never be rewritten.

For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory
layout), see the companion file
[`bot-data-formats.md`](bot-data-formats.md).

This file is forward-looking: most of what it describes is
the bot thinks, not implemented. Treat as a design reference,
not a status doc.

## Forking with the bot side staying linear

### The asymmetric situation

When work needs to happen on an older code-side base — e.g.,
0.41.1 work branched off 0.41.0 while 0.42.0 is in flight on
main — the code repo can fork without difficulty. That's a
normal git/jj branch.

The bot repo can't fork the same way: each session is a linear
conversation, and rewriting an existing session line would
falsify the historical record. The conversation that happened
is the conversation that happened; it doesn't get re-played
from a different branch base.

### chid stability across rebase

jj's two-id model is built for this scenario. Each commit has
both:

- `commit_id` — git SHA, changes on rewrite.
- `change_id` — stable across `describe` / `rebase` /
  `squash-into-self`.

`ochid:` trailers carry change_ids. So when a code-side branch
later rebases on top of another branch, change_ids are
preserved and ochid trailers in both directions still resolve.

The bot thinks the only operations that break this are
`jj squash` (folding two commits into one — collapses two
change_ids into one) and `jj abandon` (removing a commit —
loses the change_id entirely). Pure rebase is safe.

### Why not time-based linkers

Time isn't a stronger primitive than change_id:

- Clock skew between processes / repos breaks ordering.
- Time still doesn't survive rewrites any better than
  change_id does — you'd still need a stable identifier baked
  into the trailer that matches some property of the
  counterpart commit.
- change_id is jj's intended stability primitive — that's
  literally the job it exists to do.

If the change_id scheme ever shows weakness, the bot thinks
the answer is jj-side hygiene (don't `squash`-collapse during
rebase) rather than switching primitives.

### Partner bookmarks

The chosen technique for forking the code side while keeping
the bot side linear: **partner bookmarks**.

When a code-side branch needs to advance independently, create
a bookmark of the same name on the bot side starting at the
*current* bot HEAD — not at the historical bot commit paired
with the code-side branch base. The two bookmarks share a name
and advance together via `vc-x1 push`, but each carries its
own repo's state.

Other names considered and rejected:

- **mirror** — implies symmetric content. The bookmarks are
  not mirrors; they hold different content with no
  point-by-point correspondence.
- **tandem** — captures cooperation, but slightly less common
  in software vocabulary.
- **paired** — bland; "pair" can imply matching halves.
- **disjoint** / **asymmetric** — describe a property of the
  relationship, not a label for the technique.
- **yoked** — accurate metaphor but obscure.

**partner** won because it conveys cooperation without
implying duplication. Always subject to change as we live
with the term.

The asymmetric ordering between code and bot history (rebased
main commits on the code side will eventually point at bot
commits that come *before* their partner-branch pairs in bot
history) is a cosmetic wrinkle, not a correctness problem —
change_ids resolve regardless of position.

### Recovery anchor

The original main HEAD — the commit being "branched away
from" — should stay put, not be rewritten or rewound. It's
the recovery anchor for the operation. If the partner-branch
work goes wrong, main is still where it was, and recovery is
`jj abandon` on the new branch's commits.

In the 0.41.1 init+clone refactor, this means main stays at
its current 0.42.0-4 docs-capture commit (`55eadc8`) for the
duration of the side-branch work. That commit becomes
redundant after the eventual rebase — its content has been
re-landed on `init-clone-refactor` — and gets dropped during
the rebase rather than being rewound now.

aiTitle: Implement non-ff merge and trapezoidal close-out

content.content:
296	**Mitigations, in order of practical adoption:**
297	
298	1. **Project-side mirroring at merge time.** When a
299	   maintainer accepts a PR, project tooling fetches the
300	   contributor's bot repo at the referenced chid and
301	   mirrors to a project-namespace archive (e.g.
302	   `bot-archives/<contributor>-<date>-<chid>.jsonl`).
303	   Future ochid resolution falls back to the mirror if
304	   the original URL 404s. The bot thinks this is the
305	   only mitigation most projects would actually adopt —
306	   cheap, obvious upside, no friction for contributors.
307	   Same pattern as LKML's permanent archive.
308	2. **Cryptographic stapling.** Embed a hash of the bot
309	   repo's tip at submission time:
310	   `ochid: <url>#<chid>;sha256=<hash>`. Tamper-detection
311	   stays sound even if retrieval fails; doesn't recover
312	   data. Adds maintenance burden to trailer parsing.
313	3. **CI-enforced live ochid at merge.** Project CI
314	   fetches the URL, verifies it resolves with parseable
315	   content, before allowing merge. Doesn't prevent
316	   later lock/delete but ensures the merge moment was
317	   sound. Contentious in open-source culture (gates
318	   contributors on infra they don't own).
319	
320	**Opt-out via project policy.** A no-bot-context commit
321	isn't a leak — it's a project policy choice. A project
322	that wants bot transparency mandates each commit carry
323	≥1 `ochid:` trailer or an explicit
324	`No-bot-context: <reason>` trailer. Enforceable in CI,
325	identical pattern to existing `Signed-off-by:`.
326	Contributors who want privacy decline that project's
327	contribution norms; their commits go elsewhere or omit
328	the trailer where the project allows.
329	
330	**Cross-bot interop bonus.** Once URL-shaped, the
331	trailer doesn't have to point at a Claude Code repo.
332	Codex, Cursor, Aider, custom in-house tools — anyone
333	whose bot leaves a publishable session record can host
334	their own bot repo and emit ochid trailers in the same
335	format. The trailer becomes "where to find the AI
336	session that produced this commit," not "where to find
337	the Claude Code session." Strictly bigger ecosystem
338	play with no extra design cost.
339	
340	**Implication for shared-central scaling.** URL-shaped
341	per-user repos potentially short-circuit the
342	size/threshold problem documented below. Each
343	contributor pays for their own bot repo; project pays
344	for an opt-in mirror archive sized per its policy. The
345	"50 devs share one ~15G bot repo" problem becomes "50
346	devs each maintain their own ~300M bot repo, project
347	mirrors selectively."
348	
349	Trade-off: shared-central keeps cross-references local
350	and fast; per-user-via-URL needs a network fetch (or a
351	warm mirror) to resolve any non-local trailer. Pick by
352	project shape — small co-located teams favor shared;
353	distributed open-source favors per-user-via-URL.
354	
355	### Bot-side commit immutability
356	
357	Codify "never rewrite bot commits" as a hard rule. The
358	reason: change_ids on the bot side need to be permanently
359	stable for ochid trailers from the code side to resolve
360	forever. Even `jj describe` (description-only) changes the
361	commit_id, which doesn't break change_ids but does signal
362	that bot commits are mutable in a way they shouldn't be.
363	
364	Trade: any commit-level metadata fix has to be done by
365	appending a new commit, not amending the existing one.
366	Awkward but correct.
367	
368	### Bot-repo size and scaling thresholds
369	
370	Concrete measurement on a single-developer vc-x1 project
371	(2026-05-07, after `cargo clean`):
372	
373	- Code repo (excluding `.claude`): ~4M
374	- Bot repo (`.claude`): 311M
375	- Ratio: ~78:1 bot-to-code
376	
377	Cause: every Claude Code session writes a UUID-named
378	`.jsonl` file. Content is mostly unique per session —
379	git/jj packs each file's append history well, but
380	cross-file dedup is weak. So bot-repo bytes scale roughly
381	linearly with total session count.
382	
383	Linear extrapolation to shared-central multi-user (the bot
384	thinks this is a reasonable upper bound; actual savings
385	from cross-session dedup are small):
386	
387	- 5 devs: ~1.5G
388	- 20 devs: ~6G
389	- 50 devs: ~15G
390	
391	Rough operational thresholds:
392	
393	- **≤ ~10 users:** shared-central is fine.
394	- **~10-50 users:** shared-central starts hurting
395	  (multi-minute initial clone; noticeable `vc-x1 sync`
396	  pulls).
397	- **> ~50 users or > ~10G bot repo:** mitigations become
398	  necessary.
399	
400	### Monotonic-growth asymmetry
401	
402	Bot-repo size only grows. Once a session jsonl is
403	committed, the bot-side immutability rule keeps it
404	forever. This is by design (historical record), but it
405	means the bot repo has no analog of `cargo clean`:
406	code-repo working bytes can be reclaimed (build artifacts
407	come back on `cargo build`); bot-repo bytes are permanent.
408	
409	Sizing assumptions for shared-central scaling are a
410	one-way ratchet, not a steady-state cost.
411	
412	### Mitigation menu (when thresholds approach)
413	
414	None worth building preemptively. Listed so trigger points
415	have known responses:
416	
417	1. **Bot-repo pruning/archiving.** Move closed-out
418	   sessions (older than N months, or tied to commits no
419	   longer in any active branch) to an archive repo.
420	   Active bot repo stays small; archive fetched on demand
421	   when older history is referenced. The bot thinks this
422	   is the cheapest big win — recent sessions are the hot
423	   set, old ones are cold.
424	2. **Sparse fetch by trailer-referenced sessions.** When
425	   fetching the code repo, derive the set of bot-side
426	   change_ids referenced by ochids in the fetched range
427	   and fetch only those bot commits. Requires git/jj
428	   sparse-fetch + a trailer-walking step. Heavier to
429	   build but decouples clone cost from bot-repo size.
430	3. **Per-user bot repos.** The structural alternative —
431	   see "Per-user vs shared bot repo" above. Viable when
432	   shared-central pruning/sparse-fetch isn't enough.
433	4. **LFS for `.jsonl`.** Probably overkill — LFS targets
434	   binary blobs, not append-only text. Listed only
435	   because it's a known git escape hatch.
436	
437	### Tracking trigger
438	
439	Concretely measurable: the 311M baseline is the current
440	benchmark. When the shared-central bot repo crosses ~1G
441	or starts dominating clone time noticeably, that's the
442	design trigger for pruning. User count alone is not the
443	right signal; bot-repo size is.
444	
445	## Future-direction notes
446	
447	Two alternatives to the partner-bookmark technique that may
448	be worth considering once we live with the current scheme:
449	
450	### Always keep the bot repo on main
451	
452	Instead of creating a partner bookmark on the bot side for
453	each code-side branch, the bot side could be locked to a
454	single bookmark — `main` — for the entire lifetime of the
455	project. Every push would advance bot main, regardless of
456	what bookmark the code side is on.
457	
458	The bot thinks this aligns with "bot history is one linear
459	journal" more strongly than partner bookmarks do. Partner
460	bookmarks introduce *parallel* bot bookmarks even though the
461	bot history under them is still linear; an always-main
462	discipline removes that parallelism entirely.
463	
464	Trade: requires push / finalize / sync to support asymmetric
465	bookmark names per side (see next note).
466	
467	### Multi-bookmark-name support in push / finalize / sync
468	
469	Today, `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` advances the same bookmark
470	name on both code and bot sides. The "always-on-main"
471	discipline above (and other asymmetric workflows) requires
472	push / finalize / sync to take separate bookmark arguments
473	per side, e.g.:
474	
475	```
476	vc-x1 push --code-bookmark init-clone-refactor --bot-bookmark main
477	vc-x1 push -B init-clone-refactor              # bot defaults to main
478	vc-x1 push init-clone-refactor                  # both, today's behavior
479	```
480	
481	The bot thinks the right shape lands once `--scope` is
482	fully wired across the dual-repo commands — `--scope`
483	already handles "which side does this command apply to,"
484	and adding per-side bookmark arguments is a natural
485	extension.
486	
487	## Open questions worth thinking about
488	
489	- **Conflict resolution sessions: how do they get
490	  authored?** When user C resolves a merge conflict, does
491	  their bot session start a fresh chain rooted "from
492	  nowhere," or branch off one of the contributing chains?
493	  The chain-rooting question affects how the viewer renders
494	  it.
495	- **De-duplication on octopus merges.** If parents share
496	  ancestry, you'll get redundant ochids. Trim to unique
497	  tips? Keep all? The bot thinks trim, but worth deciding.
498	- **Bot-side bookmark scheme.** Per-session bookmark?
499	  Per-user-per-feature? Naming convention matters because
500	  bookmarks are how `jj git push` decides what to send.
501	- **Viewer concerns.** "Show me the sessions that
502	  contributed to this commit" is straightforward (read the
503	  trailers, walk back in each chain to the relevant range).
504	  "Show me the code commits this session contributed to" is
